So first, I'm going to give you a quick review of kind of a timeline and what we've done. I'm going to tell you a little bit about our FCS AI and education team that we put together, share with you work in progress, and then also our next steps. So as Mike noted, really the first step in bringing AI to kind of our end users was taken back in late 2020. So we had a really two-in-gen GBT was released and really made AI more accessible for everyone. We quickly saw that had the opportunity to be very, have an impact on our schools and our classrooms, our teachers and our students, and bring about a level of disruption potentially. So we formed a working group of teachers, ed tech coaches and instructional coaches, and with our district office staff, we really wanted to hear from our school-based personnel to hear what they were seeing in the classrooms, from their teachers, from their students, and we also wanted to have a dialogue with them from our side to be able to share what we were hearing from our vendors and the different applications that were coming on board. So two things that we immediately did before we're back to school in 2023, we added some language to our family engagement guide that provided guidance on AI use and academic integrity surrounding that. And we also provided some initial guidance to our schools, our ETCs, and our teachers on what was some appropriate uses of AI, what tools were out there. And then also the importance of never sharing personally identifiable information or any type of student data out on those websites. And then throughout that school year we did, we created a website or a webpage that our teachers had access to within class link. They could go to you to look at approved tools that went through our application kind of approval process that were safe for our teachers to use. And then last fall, we started offering some AI professional learning for our schools, from our district ed tech coaches, and some of our schools really jumped into that on their own as well and offered their own trainings. We also, within our Microsoft tenant, Microsoft 365, KOPOW was added, and that was a very important step because it created kind of a... a secure, close environment where even if something were to be input, it would be contained within our Microsoft tenant, so it would not go out into any type of large language model. And then this past January, we took that worker group and really expanded it into our AFFCS AI and education team.

this is looking at our Um Technology Improvement Levy: Uh, so this is all new Spending: Uh, as proposed in the Mayor's Budget through the Technology Improvement Levy Uh, which included Conference Room Mo Modernization, MicrosoftÂ Co-pilotÂ and Uh Communications, and Uh Neighborhood and Community Relations Department Enterprise Engagement Proposal: Uh, I originally was trying to balance the needs of the Um Enterprise with uh, you know, the levy pressure. Thank you so much, President Hardin and Chair Bankston and members of Council here. We are going to do a fast-paced journey through our budget presentation. So let us see if our technology works. Perfect. As you all know since really 2018, we have embarked on a critical modernization journey. Replacing outdated legacy systems with advanced cloud-based platforms, such as Microsoft D365 for accounting and operations, day force for payroll, and our GenTax system for our tax revenue system. These systems not only enhance the efficiency of our operations, but also ensure that residents and taxpayers have access to secure, intuitive,  and user-friendly services. Our transparency portal, for example, and it is an external-facing portal, it contains all kinds of frequently asked questions. And it's soon going to be powered by Microsoft Co-Pilot, greatly enhancing some user experience and allowing our residents and our folks doing business. We are going to do business with us to get answers to questions much, much faster. Our office firmly believes in the importance of investing in the business of government. When you consider the scale and the complexity of the systems we manage, rivaling that of a Fortune 1000 organization, it becomes clear that this level of investment is essential. These advancements enable us to deliver exceptional service, maintain public trust, and ensure that our systems operate as effectively and dynamically as the most frankly sophisticated private enterprises.

And then the other question related to Gavi, either… ...that is, you know, I feel like every other day I read an article about the Godfather of AI, saying we got to be careful, we should hit the brakes and slow down. And all this, and I can't help but think as we talk about these topics, Terminator and, you know, the pre-crime unit from these movie that I saw right here. All these things come to mind, right? And so, does Gavi, I, given that it's a broad coalition, I saw the map, the United States, it seems like it's all over the place, maybe, at East Coast, West Coast, with some other sprinkled in between. But are you all, or are we all taking a position as it relates to the cautious nature that some of these folks are preaching, that we should take as it relates to the advancement of AI? I will give you my personal opinion, and I'll lead the team chime in if they want. If you look at the technologies introduced in the last 30 years, and this is actually one of my slides when I showed all the advancements in AI, and if you look at all the advancements and what these technologies brought to us, including the iPhone and social media and everything, and you multiply that by 10,000... We get close to the potential benefit of AI. At the same time, if you take all the concerns that we had because of social media, cybersecurity, privacy, and everything that we learned in the last 25 years, and you multiply that by the same factor, 10,000... We have the same concern about the risk. So it is benefit and risk, and my position is, I want to make sure that we are at the leading edge of understanding and driving that technology, but at the same time, we are as fearful as possible of that technology. To make sure that we are not implementing something that is going to come back and bite us. And one of the things that we discuss in the coalition, if one of us get a black eye because of AI, all of us will be tainted by the same color. After all, all of us are government employees, all of us are government agencies, so when something happens in New York related to chat, and I try to bring something to counsel to approve a chat, I am going to be tainted question by the mishaps of some other agencies. And that is really one of the beautiful things that we have with the coalition. That we are helping each other out to figure out what is going on, and how can we be in a better position to defend and identify potential risks as a government entity versus being fragmented into cities, counties, and states, because all of us are faced with the same challenge. And on that same line is Gove AI, because I think we have sort of lifted it up as we are doing all these wonderful things, which I am glad we are taking the lead, and bringing together all of these different government agencies. But is there a policy position or a statement of policies or whatnot that Gove AI, that the coalition has taken as a release to federal legislation looking at how AI is progressing and just the thoughtfulness of it and the policies? We caught the attention of the White House Office of Technology, the Governance Office, the Homeland Security, and they are really interested in the work that we are doing, especially that we are capable or able to attract great minds throughout the nation that are willing to volunteer their time and energy to work with us. So there is a lot of conversation happening where they are trying to understand from us what we are seeing as risks so they can implement certain policies at the federal and national levels, but they cannot move as fast as we can, just getting something passed. The federal level might take years and months, or what we are doing is really implementing things in days, weeks and months. So our template AI policy was established in four months, and when we got 150 agencies drafting that and agreeing that this is something that the olcan work would. If you try to do something at the federal level, instead of four months, it is going to be four years or even more. So that is why I think the balance between what the federal level is doing and what we are doing is very interesting to them that they see that a lot of what we are seeing can influence their policies and a lot of things that they are seeing can influence what we are trying to do in a quick way.
